label: Minimalist Magazine
description: Cool, modern editorial variant with oversized typography, abundant white space, and quiet typographic hierarchy — for design publications, product blogs, and modern publishing where restraint signals quality.
signatureMotif: oversized display typography against generous white space with thin rules and quiet metadata
promptHints: Treat typography as the primary visual element — let large headlines and confident type pairings carry the design.
Use abundant white space and thin dividing rules instead of cards, shadows, or colored panels.

layouts: Use a homepage with one oversized lead headline (60-90px), a short standfirst paragraph, and a single hero image — followed by a sparse two-column index of secondary stories with metadata only.
Build article pages with a wide left margin, a single narrow reading column (~600px), and quiet bylines/dates set in small caps above the headline.
Use category pages as alphabetized or chronological indexes with thin horizontal rules between rows; let typography hierarchy alone signal importance — no badges or chips.
Compose the homepage hero as a full-bleed quiet image with the lead headline anchored bottom-left, and the rest of the page reduced to a clean type-only stack.
Use editor's-note or about pages as long-form prose with pull quotes set in display font, generous line-height (1.7+), and small-cap eyebrow labels for sections.
motifs: Off-white or near-white backgrounds (#FAFAF8 or similar) with deep ink text, rare accent color used only on links/marks.
Display serif or geometric sans for headlines paired with restrained body type at 17-19px and line-height 1.6+ for true reading comfort.
Thin (1px) horizontal rules separating sections rather than card surfaces or shadows; ample 80-120px vertical spacing between major blocks.
Small-caps or all-caps metadata at 11-12px with letter-spacing, used sparingly to mark categories, dates, and bylines — never as headings.
antiPatterns: Avoid the warm/paper feel of warm-editorial — this variant is cooler, more disciplined, more design-publication.
Never use card surfaces, drop shadows, gradients, or colored backgrounds; the design relies on typography and rules alone.
Avoid CTA-heavy or marketing-style sections that interrupt the reading rhythm; this variant prioritizes editorial trust over conversion.
Do not stack many small visual elements (chips, tags, icons) — the visual signature is restraint, not a magazine of busy widgets.
